text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with previous traumatic head injury in adolescence/Type 2 diabetes managed in primary care/mild diabetic peripheral neuropathy by history/overweight/BP well controlled per practice records/smoker (cutting down). 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Metformin 1 g twice daily (per GP).  
Atorvastatin 20 mg at night (per GP).  
Ramipril 5 mg once daily (per GP).  
Cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily.  

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, attending alone. He feels well and reports stable mood, sleep generally good, and no interim neurological complaints. He continues routine reviews with his GP for diabetes and reports fasting glucose typically in target, with a recent HbA1c he recalls as “about 49” (awaiting confirmation from the practice). There have been no intercurrent illnesses, head injuries, or medication interruptions. He does not drive. 

Seizures: He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 2 - 3 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years. He keeps a seizure diary on his phone and has not logged any events. No episodes of confusion, tongue biting, myalgia on waking, or injury. No triggers identified recently. Adherence to medication is reported as excellent, with alarms set on his phone. 

Examination: Alert, oriented, normal speech and language. Cranial nerves intact. Tone, power and coordination normal in all four limbs. Sensation: reduced vibration at the toes bilaterally consistent with mild distal neuropathy by history; otherwise light touch intact. Gait steady. No postural tremor. BP in clinic 128/76, pulse 72 regular. Weight 91 kg (BMI 29.8 kg/m²). 

Investigations: No recent antiseizure medication levels required. Last routine bloods reportedly done by GP in June 2025; I have requested copies (U&E, LFT, FBC, HbA1c, lipids, vitamin D). No recent EEG or MRI indicated in view of long-term clinical stability and absence of red flags. 

Plan:  
- Continue current antiseizure regimen unchanged given sustained remission and good tolerability.  
- Safety counselling reinforced: adherence, adequate sleep, alcohol moderation, and seizure first-aid reviewed.  
- I will liaise with the practice to confirm recent diabetes metrics; ongoing diabetes management to remain in primary care.  
- Smoking cessation advice discussed; patient considering nicotine replacement via GP.  
- Annual neurology review unless concerns arise earlier; patient to contact clinic promptly if any breakthrough events, medication issues, or new neurological symptoms.  

Yours sincerely

entities.text: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 2 - 3 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly discusses seizures. It states the patient is in long-term remission and has been seizure free for years, with no logged events in his seizure diary. The requirement for seizure-free status is at least 6 months; the phrase 'for years' clearly exceeds this. No other current seizure frequencies are provided that would override this. Therefore, the correct normalized label is 'seizure free for multiple years'.
seizure_frequency_number: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years
reference: He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 2 - 3 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

flaws.description: Gold (seizure free for multiple year) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
